title: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders
authors: Robert O. Messing, Eric J. Nestler, Matthew W. State

items.title: Genetic Contribution to Psychiatric Disorders
items.content: Psychiatric disorders are highly heritable, with genetic risk contributing significantly to disease vulnerability. This ranges from 20% to 90%, depending on the specific syndrome.
keyPoints: Psychiatric disorders have a high heritability factor.
Genetic risk comprises 20–90% of disease vulnerability.
The variability in genetic contribution depends on the specific psychiatric disorder.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · Biology of Psychiatric Disorders

items.title: Challenges and Advances in Understanding Psychiatric Illnesses
items.content: Despite challenges such as lack of well-defined neuropathology and biologic markers, recent technological advances have led to significant progress. These include neuroimaging methods, genome-wide association studies, high-throughput sequencing, and the use of patient-derived pluripotent stem cells.
keyPoints: Diagnoses are made from clinical observations using DSM-5 criteria due to lack of well-defined neuropathology and biologic markers.
Recent advances include neuroimaging, genome-wide association studies, high-throughput sequencing, and the use of patient-derived pluripotent stem cells.
Research Domain Criteria (RDoC) is an alternative diagnostic scheme focusing on core behavioral abnormalities and associated brain circuitry.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · Biology of Psychiatric Disorders

items.title: Genetic Basis of Autism Spectrum Disorders (ASDs)
items.content: Advances in genome-wide association and high-throughput sequencing studies have significantly enhanced our understanding of the genetic underpinnings of ASDs. These disorders are highly heritable with concordance rates in monozygotic twins ranging from 60–90%, indicating a strong genetic component.
keyPoints: ASDs are characterized by impaired social communication and restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior.
Concordance rates for ASDs in monozygotic twins range from 60–90%.
ASDs are highly genetically heterogeneous and inherited in a polygenic fashion.
Studies have identified about 70–250 ASD genes with false discovery rates ranging from <0.001 to <0.1.
~20–30% of ASD cases seen in clinic are accounted for by mutations or CNVs, though none individually account for >1%.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Rare Mutations and Copy Number Variations on ASD
items.content: Next-generation DNA sequencing has revealed that a substantial minority of ASD patients carry rare, often de novo, protein-damaging mutations. Additionally, about 10% of affected individuals have CNVs, which confer significant risks.
keyPoints: Rare, spontaneous (de novo), heterozygous, protein-damaging mutations are identified in a substantial minority of ASD patients.
~10% of ASD-affected individuals carry rare de novo CNVs that confer very large risks.
Mutations and CNVs account for ~20–30% of cases seen in clinic.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 1)

items.title: Biological Themes and Pathogenic Mechanisms in ASD
items.content: The discovery of pathogenic rare mutations has highlighted common biological themes, such as involvement in synaptic structure/function and transcriptional regulation. Transcriptomic studies suggest glutamatergic neurons in the mid-fetal human cortex are particularly vulnerable.
keyPoints: Many identified pathogenic mutations affect genes involved in synaptic structure/function or transcriptional/chromatin regulation.
Studies point to neurogenesis and neuronal migration as additional shared pathological mechanisms.
Transcriptomic studies highlight glutamatergic neurons in the mid-fetal human cortex as enriched for ASD genetic vulnerability.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 1)

items.title: Nucleic Acid Targeting in Neurodevelopmental Disorders
items.content: Emerging therapies using nucleic acid targeting, such as CRISPR-based treatments and antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs), show promise for treating severe phenotypes in neurodevelopmental disorders with highly penetrant coding mutations.
keyPoints: Nucleic acid targeting strategies are being explored for intellectual disability syndromes like Angelman syndrome and SHANK3 deletion/Phelan-McDermid syndrome, which may also present features of ASD.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 2)

items.title: Genetic Discoveries in Schizophrenia and Bipolar Disorder
items.content: Large-scale genetic studies have identified several hundred genomic regions associated with schizophrenia and bipolar disorder through genome-wide association studies, highlighting the role of common inherited polymorphisms.
keyPoints: Several hundred distinct genomic regions marked by single nucleotide polymorphisms are linked to schizophrenia, some also relevant for bipolar disorder. Notable genes include CACNA1C and CACNB2.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 2)

items.title: Phenotypic Pleiotropy in Psychiatric Genetics
items.content: Genetic studies reveal that individual risk genes can be associated with multiple psychiatric disorders, illustrating the complexity of these conditions and the influence of various factors on phenotypic outcomes.
keyPoints: NRXN1 deletions are linked to ASD, schizophrenia, intellectual disability, epilepsy. CACNA1C polymorphisms are associated with both schizophrenia and bipolar disorder. Large-effect CNVs show overlap in phenotypes like ASD, schizophrenia, and bipolar disorder.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 2)

items.title: Lithium's Mechanism in Bipolar Disorder
items.content: Lithium is effective for bipolar disorder by competing with magnesium to inhibit magnesium-dependent enzymes, including GSK3 $ \beta $ and protein kinase C. This has spurred research into developing inhibitors of these targets as potential treatments.
keyPoints: Lithium inhibits magnesium-dependent enzymes like GSK3 $ \beta $ and protein kinase C.
Research is ongoing to develop GSK3 $ \beta $ or protein kinase C inhibitors for mood disorders.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 1)

items.title: Antidepressants and Neuroplasticity
items.content: Chronic administration of antidepressants may reverse hippocampal atrophy by increasing neurotrophic factors like BDNF, potentially reversing stress-induced decreases in new neuron generation.
keyPoints: Long-term antidepressant use is needed for effects.
Antidepressants might increase BDNF or other neurotrophic factors to counteract hippocampal atrophy.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 1)

items.title: Ketamine's Rapid Antidepressant Action
items.content: Ketamine, a noncompetitive NMDA receptor antagonist, provides rapid antidepressant effects in treatment-resistant patients. Its mechanism involves activation of TORC1 or BDNF signaling pathways.
keyPoints: Ketamine acts as a noncompetitive NMDA receptor antagonist.
Rapid antidepressant effects observed with minimal side effects at lower doses.
Antidepressant action may involve TORC1 or BDNF activation.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 1)

items.title: Mechanisms of Opioid Tolerance and Dependence
items.content: Opioids like morphine inhibit adenylyl cyclase via $ G_{i/o} $ proteins, reducing cAMP production. This leads to PKA activation and CREB transcription factor activation. Chronic opioid use results in upregulation of ACs and PKA, increasing CREB activity, contributing to craving and withdrawal symptoms.
keyPoints: Inhibition of adenylyl cyclase (AC) reduces cyclic AMP (cAMP).
Protein kinase A (PKA) is activated, leading to activation of the transcription factor CREB.
Upregulation occurs in CNS regions like locus coeruleus, periaqueductal gray, ventral tegmental area, and nucleus accumbens.
Contributes to opioid craving and withdrawal signs.
Morphine causes prolonged receptor activation due to weak induction of $ \mu $-opioid receptor desensitization and endocytosis, unlike endogenous opioids.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 2)

items.title: Differences Between Endogenous Opioids and Morphine in Tolerance
items.content: Endogenous opioid peptides do not lead to tolerance or dependence as morphine does. This difference is due to the fact that morphine and similar drugs are weak inducers of $ \mu $-opioid receptor desensitization and endocytosis, resulting in prolonged receptor activation and AC inhibition, which stimulates cAMP-CREB signaling upregulation.
keyPoints: Endogenous opioids do not produce tolerance or dependence.
Morphine is a weak inducer of $ \mu $-opioid receptor desensitization and endocytosis.
Prolonged receptor activation by morphine leads to AC inhibition.
This prolonged effect stimulates upregulation of cAMP-CREB signaling, characteristic of opioid dependence.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 2)

items.title: Neural Circuitry and Depression
items.content: Advances in brain imaging and optogenetics have identified key neural circuits involved in depression, particularly within the limbic system. These include regions such as the NAc, amygdala, hippocampus, and prefrontal cortex. Altered activity patterns, like increased amygdala activation by negative stimuli and reduced NAc response to rewards, are characteristic of depression.
keyPoints: Limbic circuitry is crucial for mood regulation in depression.
Increased amygdala activation by negative stimuli.
Reduced activation of the NAc by rewarding stimuli.
Hyperactivity in subgenual area 25 of the anterior cingulate cortex.
Deep brain stimulation (DBS) targeting NAc or subgenual area 25 shows therapeutic potential.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 1)

items.title: Schizophrenia: Structural and Functional Brain Changes
items.content: Imaging studies in schizophrenia reveal structural changes such as ventricular enlargement, reduced cortical and subcortical gray matter, and altered connectivity. Functionally, there is decreased activity in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex during rest and cognitive tasks, with evidence pointing to synaptic pruning involving microglia.
keyPoints: Enlargement of the ventricular system and reduction of cortical/subcortical gray matter.
Reduced metabolic activity in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex.
Impaired structural and task-related functional connectivity, mainly in frontal/temporal lobes.
Reduction in cortical thickness associated with increased cell packing density and reduced neuropil.
Reduced expression of GAD1 gene in specific interneurons, affecting GABA synthesis.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 1)

items.title: Genetic Insights into Schizophrenia
items.content: Recent genome-wide association studies highlight the role of synaptic pruning and complement component 4 variations in schizophrenia. The C4A/C4B ratio is implicated, with increased expression potentially leading to excessive synaptic pruning.
keyPoints: Synaptic pruning involving microglia as a potential mechanism.
Variations in C4A and C4B expression linked to schizophrenia risk.
Increased C4A expression may result in excessive synaptic pruning.
Strongest effect haplotype increases risk with an odds ratio of <1.3.
Having a sibling with schizophrenia increases risk approximately tenfold.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 1)

items.title: Mechanisms of Addiction: Dopamine's Role in Reward Circuitry
items.content: Drugs of abuse primarily affect the brain's reward regions, particularly by enhancing dopamine neurotransmission. Midbrain dopamine neurons in the ventral tegmental area (VTA) are key players, responding to natural rewards and expectations thereof. Drugs converge on these pathways, promoting dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens (NAc) and other limbic structures, which results in powerful rewarding signals that can lead to addiction.
keyPoints: Dopamine neurons in the VTA are activated by natural rewards or their expectation.
Drugs of abuse enhance dopamine neurotransmission in the NAc and other limbic targets.
This enhancement leads to powerful rewarding signals, promoting addiction.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 2)

items.title: Pathologic Adaptations in Addiction: Tolerance, Sensitization, and Impaired Executive Function
items.content: Addiction involves three major pathologic adaptations. First, drugs induce tolerance within reward circuits and heightened activity in stress circuits, leading to increased drug intake and negative emotional states during withdrawal that can trigger relapse. Second, there is sensitization to the rewarding effects of drugs and associated cues, which can prompt relapse even after prolonged abstinence. Third, executive function is impaired, increasing impulsivity and compulsivity, further promoting relapse.
keyPoints: Tolerance in reward circuits and increased activity in stress circuits promote escalating drug intake.
Sensitization to drugs' rewarding effects and associated cues can trigger relapse.
Impaired executive function increases impulsivity and compulsivity, contributing to relapse.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 2)

items.title: Peripheral Cytokines as Biomarkers in Depression
items.content: A subset of depressed patients shows elevated blood levels of proinflammatory cytokines such as interleukin 6 (IL-6) and tumor necrosis factor $\alpha$ (TNF-$\alpha$). This suggests the potential use of these cytokines as biomarkers for a subtype of depression, guiding both diagnosis and treatment strategies.
keyPoints: Elevated blood levels of IL-6 and TNF-$\alpha$ in some depressed patients
Peripheral cytokines may serve as biomarkers for specific subtypes of depression
Development of antidepressants targeting specific cytokine actions is a potential therapeutic strategy
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROINFLAMMATION | CONCLUSIONS

items.title: Proinflammatory Signaling and Alcohol Addiction
items.content: Alcohol use disorder involves impaired innate immunity and increased levels of proinflammatory cytokines, which affect brain function. Research has identified a network of neuroimmune proteins involved in alcohol consumption regulation, leading to the development of anti-inflammatory medications that reduce alcohol intake.
keyPoints: Impaired innate immunity and elevated circulating proinflammatory cytokines are linked with alcohol use disorder
Key immune-related genes include MCP-1, CCL3, beta-2 microglobulin, CD14, IL-1 receptor antagonist, TLR3, TLR7, cathepsins S and F
Anti-inflammatory medications like phosphodiesterase 4 antagonists and PPAR agonists can reduce alcohol intake in animal models
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROINFLAMMATION | CONCLUSIONS

items.question: Which percentage range represents the genetic risk contributing to psychiatric disorders?
items.explanation: Genetic risk comprises 20–90% of disease vulnerability in psychiatric disorders.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · Biology of Psychiatric Disorders

items.question: What is the primary method currently used for diagnosing psychiatric disorders?
items.explanation: Diagnoses are made from clinical observations using criteria in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition (DSM-5).
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · Biology of Psychiatric Disorders

items.question: What is a key limitation in understanding mental illness mentioned in the passage?
options: Inherent limitations of animal models for certain disorders
items.explanation: Animal models have inherent limitations for disorders defined largely by behavioral abnormalities.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · Biology of Psychiatric Disorders

items.question: Which type of genetic variation is primarily responsible for the heritability of autism spectrum disorders (ASDs)?
options: Common alleles with large individual effects.
Copy number variations in a small subset of individuals.
Polygenic inheritance involving many common alleles.
items.explanation: ASDs are mainly inherited in a polygenic fashion, conferred by a conspiracy of alleles that are common in the population and carry small individual effects.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 1)

items.question: What is the estimated range of ASD cases accounted for by mutations in individual genes or gene-rich genomic regions?
items.explanation: Mutations in individual genes or gene-rich genomic regions account for ~20–30% of cases of ASD seen in clinic.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 1)

items.question: Which biological themes have emerged from the study of pathogenic rare mutations in ASD?
options: Synaptic structure or function and transcriptional regulation.
items.explanation: Many of the identified pathogenic rare mutations are in genes that encode proteins involved in synaptic structure or function, or in transcriptional and chromatin regulation.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 1)

items.question: What is a common feature among individuals with fragile X syndrome or tuberous sclerosis regarding ASD?
options: They have no genetic overlap with idiopathic ASD.
They show elevated rates of ASD and may carry mutations found in idiopathic ASD patients.
They are unaffected by the same risk genes as those with idiopathic ASD.
Their condition is primarily caused by environmental factors.
items.explanation: Individuals with fragile X syndrome or tuberous sclerosis show elevated rates of ASD, and mutations in the causal genes may be found in patients who present with otherwise idiopathic ASD.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 1)

items.question: Which genetic approach has shown remarkable success in early intervention for spinal muscular atrophy?
items.explanation: Remarkable success with early intervention in spinal muscular atrophy has been achieved using CRISPR-based therapies or ASOs.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 2)

items.question: What type of mutations have primarily contributed to gene discovery for schizophrenia and bipolar disorder?
items.explanation: Gene discovery for schizophrenia and bipolar disorder has largely resulted from genome-wide association studies of common inherited polymorphisms.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 2)

items.question: Which gene cluster is associated with susceptibility to nicotine and alcohol addiction?
items.explanation: The CHRNA5-A3-B4 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor gene cluster on chromosome 15 is associated with nicotine and alcohol addiction.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 2)

items.question: What does phenotypic pleiotropy refer to in the context of psychiatric disorders?
options: The presence of multiple genetic mutations causing a single disorder
A single genetic mutation leading to different disorders across individuals
The identification of common polymorphisms in all psychiatric disorders
The use of CRISPR-based therapies for various conditions
items.explanation: Phenotypic pleiotropy refers to individual risk genes being associated with multiple psychiatric and neurodevelopmental syndromes.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 2)

items.question: Which enzyme does lithium inhibit that is involved in phosphoinositide signaling?
items.explanation: Lithium inhibits GSK3 $ \beta $, which is involved in phosphoinositide signaling.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary action of tricyclic antidepressants like imipramine?
items.explanation: Tricyclic antidepressants like imipramine inhibit serotonin and/or norepinephrine reuptake.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 1)

items.question: What is a proposed mechanism by which chronic antidepressant administration might reverse hippocampal atrophy?
items.explanation: Chronic antidepressant administration might reverse hippocampal atrophy by increasing BDNF.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 1)

items.question: Which receptor does ketamine act as a noncompetitive antagonist of?
items.explanation: Ketamine acts as a noncompetitive antagonist of NMDA glutamate receptors.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 1)

items.question: Which proteins are involved in the inhibition of adenylyl cyclase, leading to reduced cyclic AMP production?
items.explanation: $ G_{i/o} $ proteins inhibit adenylyl cyclase, reducing cAMP production.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 2)

items.question: Which transcription factor is activated as a result of reduced cyclic AMP levels?
items.explanation: Reduced cAMP leads to activation of the transcription factor CREB.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 2)

items.question: Which brain regions are involved in the upregulation of cAMP-CREB signaling related to opioid craving and withdrawal?
options: Locus coeruleus, periaqueductal gray, ventral tegmental area
items.explanation: The locus coeruleus, periaqueductal gray, and ventral tegmental area are involved in cAMP-CREB signaling related to opioid effects.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 2)

items.question: Which brain regions are integral to the neural circuitry of mood within the limbic system?
options: Basal ganglia, medulla oblongata, pons, midbrain
Hypothalamus, pituitary gland, pineal gland, corpus callosum
items.explanation: The NAc, amygdala, hippocampus, and regions of prefrontal cortex are integral to the neural circuitry of mood within the limbic system.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 1)

items.question: Which technique allows control of specific types of neurons in defined circuits using light?
items.explanation: Optogenetics allows control of specific types of neurons in defined circuits using light.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 1)

items.question: What is the principal inhibitory neurotransmitter synthesized by glutamic acid decarboxylase 1 (GAD1) in the brain?
items.explanation: The principal inhibitory neurotransmitter synthesized by GAD1 is gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA).
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging technique has revealed increased activation of the amygdala by negative stimuli in depression?
items.explanation: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has revealed increased activation of the amygdala by negative stimuli in depression.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 1)

items.question: Which brain region's neurons function as rheostats of reward, being activated by natural rewards or their expectation?
items.explanation: Midbrain dopamine neurons in the VTA function as rheostats of reward.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 2)

items.question: What is one major pathologic adaptation described that contributes to addiction by promoting escalating drug intake?
options: Increased activity in limbic stress circuits
Reduced gray matter in the prefrontal cortex
items.explanation: Drugs produce tolerance in reward circuits and increased activity in limbic stress circuits, promoting escalating drug intake.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 2)

items.question: Which brain regions show reduced gray matter in patients who abuse alcohol or psychostimulants?
items.explanation: Patients show reduced gray matter in the prefrontal cortex and reduced activity in anterior cingulate and orbitofrontal cortex.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 2)

items.question: Which cytokine is associated with increased neuronal excitability, dopamine release, and locomotor activity when administered into the VTA?
items.explanation: MCP-1 modulates neurotransmitter release, increases neuronal excitability, and promotes dopamine release when administered into the VTA.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROINFLAMMATION | CONCLUSIONS

items.question: Which type of receptors are targeted by agonists that reduce alcohol intake in animal studies?
items.explanation: Agonists of PPARs, which repress key inflammatory signaling molecules, have been shown to reduce alcohol intake in animals.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROINFLAMMATION | CONCLUSIONS

items.question: What is a potential future application of peripheral cytokines in psychiatry?
items.explanation: Peripheral cytokines are being considered as biomarkers for a subtype of depression, with potential utility in developing new antidepressants.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROINFLAMMATION | CONCLUSIONS

items.statement: The DSM classification accurately reflects the underlying biology of psychiatric illnesses.
items.explanation: There is increasing agreement that the DSM does not accurately reflect the underlying biology.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · Biology of Psychiatric Disorders

items.statement: Recent technological advances have led to significant progress in understanding the genetic basis of psychiatric disorders.
items.explanation: Technological advances and large patient cohorts have contributed to progress in studying genetics.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · Biology of Psychiatric Disorders

items.statement: The majority of ASD cases can be attributed to a single genetic mutation.
items.explanation: No individual gene or genomic region accounts for more than ~1% of ASD cases.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 1)

items.statement: Concordance rates in monozygotic twins for ASDs are significantly higher than those in dizygotic twins.
items.explanation: Concordance rates in monozygotic twins range from 60–90%, which is a four- to sixfold increase compared to dizygotic twins and siblings.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 1)

items.statement: Rare large-effect mutations have not been identified in schizophrenia.
items.explanation: As the scale of high-throughput sequencing studies has expanded, rare large-effect mutations have also been identified in schizophrenia.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 2)

items.statement: The CHRNA5-A3-B4 gene cluster is associated with both schizophrenia and bipolar disorder.
items.explanation: The CHRNA5-A3-B4 gene cluster is specifically associated with nicotine and alcohol addiction, not directly with schizophrenia or bipolar disorder.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 2)

items.statement: The monoamine hypothesis has been substantiated as the sole cause of depression.
items.explanation: The monoamine hypothesis has not been substantiated as the sole cause of depression.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 1)

items.statement: Ketamine's antidepressant effects are long-lasting without repeated administration.
items.explanation: The response to ketamine is transient, requiring approaches like repeated delivery.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 1)

items.statement: Endogenous opioid peptides produce tolerance and dependence similar to morphine.
items.explanation: Endogenous opioids do not produce tolerance and dependence, unlike morphine.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 2)

items.statement: Morphine causes prolonged receptor activation by inducing $ μ $-opioid receptor desensitization.
items.explanation: Morphine is a weak inducer of $ μ $-opioid receptor desensitization, causing prolonged receptor activation.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 2)

items.statement: Deep brain stimulation (DBS) trials have been conducted on subgenual area 25 for therapeutic effects in severe depression.
items.explanation: Trials of deep brain stimulation (DBS) of either the NAc or subgenual area 25 appear to be therapeutic in some severely depressed individuals.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 1)

items.statement: Schizophrenia is characterized by increased cortical thickness and reduced cell packing density.
items.explanation: Schizophrenia shows a reduction in cortical thickness associated with increased cell packing density, not the other way around.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 1)

items.statement: Drugs of abuse initially target neurotransmitter receptors to alter neurotransmission.
items.explanation: Drugs of abuse have initial actions at different classes of ion channels, neurotransmitter receptors, or neurotransmitter transporters.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 2)

items.statement: Repeated drug administration leads to decreased activity in limbic stress circuits.
items.explanation: Repeated drug administration increases activity in limbic stress circuits, promoting escalating drug intake.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 2)

items.statement: Proinflammatory signaling in the brain has been linked to both depression and addiction.
items.explanation: The passage discusses links between proinflammatory signaling, depression, and alcohol addiction.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROINFLAMMATION | CONCLUSIONS

items.statement: Astrocytes and microglia are not involved in the mechanisms by which proinflammatory cytokines impair brain function.
items.explanation: The passage mentions a role for astrocytes and microglia in how proinflammatory cytokines affect brain function.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROINFLAMMATION | CONCLUSIONS

items.assertion: The Research Domain Criteria (RDoC) system classifies mental illness based on core behavioral abnormalities.
items.reason: This classification is associated with the brain circuitry that controls these behavioral domains.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · Biology of Psychiatric Disorders

items.assertion: The identification of germline genetic risk allows for experiments addressing temporal sequencing in the development of psychiatric disorders.
items.reason: Germline genetic risk is present before brain development, theoretically enabling studies on cause versus effect.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why germline genetic risk allows for temporal sequencing experiments.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 1)

items.assertion: Studies of tens of thousands of individuals have identified numerous associations meeting gold-standard genome-wide statistical thresholds.
items.reason: The increments of increased risk for any common ASD risk allele are so modest that only a handful of associations meet these thresholds.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because only a handful of associations have been identified, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 1)

items.assertion: Genome-wide association studies have been pivotal in identifying risk loci for schizophrenia.
items.reason: These studies leverage common inherited polymorphisms to detect genetic associations with the disorder.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why genome-wide association studies are important for identifying risk loci in schizophrenia.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 2)

items.assertion: The same genetic mutation can lead to different disorders across individuals.
items.reason: This is due to the pleiotropic nature of psychiatric disorder genes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why a single mutation may result in various disorders.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · NEUROGENETICS (part 2)

items.assertion: Ketamine's antidepressant effects are mediated by NMDA receptor antagonism.
items.reason: The mechanism underlying ketamine's antidepressant action is not known, and its role as an NMDA receptor antagonist has been questioned.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the mechanism of ketamine's effects is uncertain and its role as an NMDA receptor antagonist is in question.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 1)

items.assertion: Psychedelic drugs are being studied for their potential to treat depression.
items.reason: They act as partial agonists of serotonin $ 5-HT_{2A} $ receptors and promote serotonin release.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason explaining the mechanism by which psychedelic drugs might be effective.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION (part 1)

items.assertion: Genome-wide association studies suggest synaptic pruning as a potential mechanism in schizophrenia.
items.reason: Variations in the relative expression of complement component 4 isotypes, C4A and C4B, are associated with schizophrenia risk.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 1)

items.assertion: The strongest effect haplotype in humans accounts for a significant increase in schizophrenia risk.
items.reason: Having a sibling with schizophrenia increases risk approximately tenfold.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the strongest effect haplotype only slightly increases risk, whereas having a sibling significantly increases it.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 1)

items.assertion: Damage to cortical areas contributes to addiction by impairing decision-making.
items.reason: Reduced gray matter in the prefrontal cortex and reduced activity in anterior cingulate and orbitofrontal cortex are observed in patients who abuse alcohol or psychostimulants.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Biology of Psychiatric Disorders · SYSTEMS NEUROSCIENCE (part 2)
